title: integrálás
text:
határozatlan integrál

anti-derivált, primitív függvény


   \left( \int f\right)'=f




tulajdonságok

C,D\in\mathbb{R}

\begin{gather*}
   \int\left( Cf+g\right) =C\int{f}+\int{g}\\
   \int C\text{d}x =  Cx+D\\
   \int x^{C}\text{d}x =  \frac{x^{C+1}}{C+1}+D\hspace{1cm} C\neq-1\\
   \int{\sin}=-\cos+C\\
   \int{\cos}=\sin+C\\
   \int \frac{1}{x}\text{d}x=\log{|x|}+C\hspace{1cm}x\neq0\\
   \int C^x\text{d}x=\frac{C^x}{\log{C}}+D\hspace{1cm}C>0,C\neq1\\
   \int\left(f'g+fg'\right)=fg+C\\
   \int f(g)g'=\left( \int f \right)(g)+C
\end{gather*}



számoljuk ki a következő integrálokat

\int xe^{x^2}\text{d}x

válasz: \begin{gather*}
\left(e^{x^2}\right){'}=2xe^{x^2} \ \ \rightarrow \  \frac{e^{x^2}}{2}+C
\end{gather*}

\int \sin(x)e^x\text{d}x

válasz: \begin{gather*}
\text{(1)}\ \ \int \sin(x)e^{x}\text{d}x=-\cos(x)e^{x}-\int -\cos(x)e^{x}\text{d}x=\\
-\cos(x)e^{x}+\int \cos(x)e^{x}\text{d}x\\
\text{(2)}\ \ \int \sin(x)e^{x}\text{d}x=\sin(x)e^{x}-\int \cos(x)e^{x}\text{d}x\\
\text{(1)+(2)}\ \rightarrow \\
\int \sin(x)e^{x}\text{d}x=\frac{\sin(x)-\cos(x)}{2}e^{x}+C
\end{gather*}

\int \cos^3(x)\text{d}x

válasz: \begin{gather*}
\text{helyettesítés: }\ u=sin(x)\\
\int \cos^3(x)\text{d}x=\int (1-\sin^2(x))\cos(x)\text{d}x=\\
\int 1-u^2\text{d}u=u-\frac{u^3}{3}+C=\sin(x)-\frac{\sin^3(x)}{3}+C
\end{gather*}

\int e^{au}\sin(u)\text{d}u\ \ 0\neq a\in\mathbb{R}

válasz: \begin{gather*}
\text{parciális integrálás: }\ f=e^{au},\ \ g'=\sin(u)\\
\int e^{au}\sin(u)\text{d}u=-e^{au}\cos(u)-\int ae^{au}(-\cos(u))\text{d}u=\\
=-e^{au}\cos(u)+a\int e^{au}\cos(u)\text{d}u\\
\text{parciális integrálás: }\ f'=e^{au},\ \ g=\sin(u)\\
\int e^{au}\sin(u)\text{d}u=\frac{e^{au}}{a}\sin(u)-\int \frac{e^{au}}{a}\cos(u)\text{d}u=\\
\frac{e^{au}}{a}\sin(u)-\frac{1}{a}\int e^{au}\cos(u)\text{d}u\\
\text{szorozzuk meg $a^2$-el a másodikat és adjuk össze az elsővel}\\
(1+a^2)\int e^{au}\sin(u)\text{d}u=ae^{au}\sin(u)-e^{au}\cos(u) \ \ \implies\\
\int e^{au}\sin(u)\text{d}u=\frac{e^{au}\left({a\sin(u)-\cos(u)}\right)}{1+a^2}
\end{gather*}

we want to compute the value of the integral:

title: numint/concepts_draft
text:
interpolational quadrature formulas

more specifically: for a given f and nodes a=t_1,\ldots ,t_n=b and values f(t_1),\ldots,f(t_n) we determine the Lagrange interpolational polynomial P and its exact integral value will be used to approximate \int_{a}^{b} f(t)\text{d}t.

\begin{gather*}
f(t) \approx P(t)\\
\int_{a}^{b} f(t)\text{d}t \approx \int_{a}^{b} P(t)\text{d}t
\end{gather*}

we will learn what are the weights by using 1,2,3 nodes and how the corresponding quadrature rules are behaving.

\infty-norm
\begin{gather*}
\vert(Ax)_{i}\vert=
\vert\sum_{j} a_{ij} x_{j}\vert\le
\sum_{j} \vert a_{ij}\vert \vert x_{j}\vert\le
\sum_{j} \vert a_{ij}\vert \Vert x\Vert_{\infty}\le
\Vert A\Vert_{\infty} \vert \Vert x\Vert_{\infty}
\end{gather*} if the maximum is attained in the i-th row then for the sign vector of that row equality holds.

title: norm/notes_norm
text:
1-norm
\begin{gather*}
\sum_{i}\vert(Ax)_{i}\vert\le
\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \vert a_{ij}\vert \vert x_{j}\vert=
\sum_{j} \vert x_{j}\vert \sum_{i} \vert a_{ij}\vert \le
\sum_{j} \vert x_{j}\vert \Vert A\Vert_{1} \le
\Vert x\Vert_{1} \Vert A\Vert_{1}
\end{gather*} if the maximum is attained in the j-th column then for the vector e_{j} the two sides are equal.

title: interpolation/concepts_draft
text:
for a given set of 2d points (t_{1},f_{1}),\ldots,(t_{n},f_{n}) we are searching the simplest (minimal degree) P polynomial that interpolates the points: P(t_{k})=f_{k}\ \ \ k=1,\ldots ,n.
terminology: the elements of t are called interpolation nodes/node points, the elements of f are called (function) values/node values.
naturally the point set cannot have two equal first coordinates (t_{i}=t_{j}) with different second coordinates (f_{i}\neq f_{j}).
for 1 points a 0-th order, 2 points an at most 1-st order interpolation is clearly exists - but how to proceed to larger number of points?
n points n pieces of information. we may expect that these n pieces will determine n real numbers, a polynomial of degree at most n-1, which will be the interpolational polynomial.
there are several approaches

setting up an n\times n linear system of equations 
a_{n-1}t_{k}^{n-1}+\ldots+a_{1}t_{k}+a_{0}=f_{k}\ \ \ k=1,\ldots,n

applying least square approximation for \varphi_{k}(t)=t^k, \ \ k=0,\ldots,n-1, for if it is possible to achieve the zero error, then the Gauss normal equation will give us the appropriate coefficients.
both of these (eventually equivalent) approaches has complexity {\cal O}(n^3) and it is not so straightforward to apply them for a dynamically extending point set.

we will learn later that there is a more efficient {\cal O}(n^2) complexity and dynamically extendable approach: the Newton divided difference method.
first we will state a theorem about the existence and uniqueness.







ImportantLagrange’s main theorem



For a given set of pairwise different points (t_{1},f_{1}),\ldots,(t_{n},f_{n}), there is a unique polynomial P with: 
P(t_{k})=f_{k}\ \ \ (k=1,\ldots,n)\ \ \text{and}\ \ \deg(P)\le n-1




for more details read this
at this stage we learned that the least square approach (even without A^{T}A) will give us the unique solution…

title: interpolation/concepts_draft
text:
intro

search for the polynomial that interpolates the points t=[-1,0,1],\ \ f=[2,3,1].
let’s imagine the polynomial in the form of p(x)=a_2(x+1)x+a_1x+a_0. here we included some information about the first coordinates into the polynomial.







Nonedetails






\begin{gather*}
p(-1)=2 \ \ \Longrightarrow \ \ a_0=2\\
p(x)=a_2(x+1)x+a_1(x+1)+2\\
p(0)=3 \ \ \Longrightarrow \ \ a_1=1\\
p(x)=a_2(x+1)x+(x+1)+2\\
p(1)=1 \ \ \Longrightarrow \ \ a_2=-\frac{3}{2}\\
p(x)=-\frac{3}{2}(x+1)x+(x+1)+2\\
\end{gather*}





observe that at each step we solved a one variable linear equation, as in the case of triangular form linear systems
this seemingly ad-hoc approach is transformed to an algorithm

title: interpolation/concepts_draft
text:
the method (how?)

the scheme of the divided difference method

\begin{array}{c|cccccc}
t_1 & f^{~(0)}_{1} & & & & & \\
& & f^{~(1)}_{1} & & & & \\
t_2 & f^{~(0)}_{2} && f^{~(2)}_{1} & & & \\
& & f^{~(1)}_{2} & & \ddots & &\\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & f^{~(n-2)}_{1} & \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & f^{~(n-1)}_{1} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & f^{~(n-2)}_{2} & \\
& & f^{~(1)}_{n-2} & & \mathinner{\kern{1.2mu}\raisebox{2mu}{.}\kern{3mu}\raisebox{7.4mu}{.}\kern{3mu}\raisebox{12.8mu}{.}\kern{1.2mu}}& & \\
t_{n-1} & f^{~(0)}_{n-1} & & f^{~(2)}_{n-2} & & & \\
& & f^{~(1)}_{n-1} & & & & \\
t_n & f^{~(0)}_{n} & & & & &
\end{array}







Importantthe computational rule



We start with the t and the f^{~(0)} columns, the the missing entries, for j=1\ldots n-1 and i=1\ldots n-j are computed by 
f_{i}^{~(j)}=\frac{f_{i}^{~(j-1)}-f_{i+1}^{~(j-1)}}{t_i-t_{i+j}} \ \ \ \ (*)









Importantthe polynomial(s)



Suppose that we filled in the table according to the rule (*). Then 
\begin{align*}
P(t)&=f^{~(0)}_{1}+f^{~(1)}_{1}(t-t_1)+f^{~(2)}_{1}(t-t_1)(t-t_2)+\ldots\\
&+f^{~(n-2)}_{1}(t-t_1)\ldots (t-t_{n-2})+f^{~(n-1)}_{1}(t-t_1)\ldots (t-t_{n-1})
\end{align*}
 and 
\begin{align*}
P(t)&=f^{~(0)}_{n}+f^{~(1)}_{n-1}(t-t_n)+f^{~(2)}_{n-2}(t-t_n)(t-t_{n-1})+\cdots\\
&+f^{~(n-2)}_{2}(t-t_n)\cdots (t-t_3)+f^{~(n-1)}_{1}(t-t_n)\cdots (t-t_2)
\end{align*}
 are interpolational polynomials for the data t,f.

text:
problem description
We have a set of points and a set of conditions at the points: 
\begin{array}{rcccccl}
& t_1            & t_2          & t_3       & \dots & t_n       & \\
\hline
& f_{1}^{(0)}      & f_{2}^{(0)}    & f_{3}^{(0)} & \dots & f_{n}^{(0)} &\\
& f_{1}^{(1)}      & f_{2}^{(1)}    & f_{3}^{(1)} & \dots & f_{n}^{(1)} &\\
& f_{1}^{(2)}      & f_{2}^{(2)}    & f_{3}^{(2)}  & \dots & f_{n}^{(2)} &\\
&\vdots          & \vdots           & \vdots       &       & \vdots     &\\
&f_{1}^{(m_1-1)}     & f_{2}^{(m_2-1)}  & f_{3}^{(m_3-1)} &\dots & f_{n}^{(m_n-1)}&\\
\end{array}

We are looking for a polynomial H(t), for which 
H^{~(j)}(t_i)=f_{i}^{(j)} \quad \quad i=1,\dots,n, \quad j=0,\dots ,m_i-1.
 Let m=\sum_{k=1}^n m_k, the number of conditions.






Important



The problem of Hermite-interpolation can be solved uniquely in the space of at most m-1 degree polynomials.



From the conditions, we can set up a uniquely solvable m\times m linear system, for the details see this at page (around) 18.
or

title: interpolation/concepts_draft
text:
computation

How to compute the coefficients of H?
It is possible to modify the Newton divided difference method for Lagrange polynomials to get them.
The node points column (t) should contain the t_k’s with multiplicity. As many conditions assigned to a specific as many times we should include it. If the first derivative is given, then we must include it 2 times, and so on.
The corresponding computational formula is modified, in order to handle the “division by zero” case







ImportantHermite-rule




f_{i}^{~(j)}=
\begin{cases}
\frac{f_{i}^{~(j-1)}-f_{i+1}^{~(j-1)}}{t_i-t_{i+j}} \ \ \text{if}\ t_i \neq t_{i+j}\\
\frac{f(t_{i})^{(j)}}{j!} \ \ \text{otherwise}\\
\end{cases}









Important



If the node points are set up with appropriate multiplicity and the modified computational rule used to fill in the values of the scheme then the polynomial in the final table fulfills the conditions. (The proof is omitted.)

applications

tangent line formula

For a given real valued, differentiable function find the equation of its tangential line at t_0!
We are looking for a Hermite polynomial of degree at most 1, for which H(t_0)=f(t_0) and H~'(t_0)=f~'(t_0)


\begin{array}{r|rr}
t_0 &  f(t_0) \\
& & f~'(t_0) \\
t_0 & f(t_0)
\end{array}


then H(t)=f(t_0)+f~'(t_0)(t-t_0)

error of the linear interpolation

let f\in C^{2}[A,B] and M_2=\max_{t\in[A,B]} |f{~''}(t)|
then for the linear interpolation P of f on [a,b]\subset [A,B] we have:







Important




\vert f(t)-P(t)\vert \leq \frac {M_2}{8}(b-a)^2, \quad\quad
t\in [a,b]




increasing the number of subintervals results in smaller error.
dividing the “large” [A,B] interval int n equal pieces (and using the same “global” M_2) the error for one [a,b] subinterval:


\vert f(t)-P_{[a,b]}\vert \leq \frac {M_2}{8}\frac{(B-A)^2}{n^2}, \quad\quad
t\in [a,b]


doubling the points = halving the length will result 4 times better approximation
pro: simple
con: sometimes for very high precision we need large number of points, but at least it works

definition

strictly diagonally dominant
a matrix A is called sdd if for each |a_{ii}| > \sum_{j\neq i}|a_{ij}| for each i

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
proposition

if A is sdd the A=LU
the key is to understand how this property is preserved during gaussian elimination.
it is enough to consider two vectors


\begin{gather*}
x\ \ \text{with} \ \ \ |x_1|>\sum_{i>1} |x_{i}| \\
y\ \ \text{with} \ \ \ |y_j|>\sum_{i\neq j} |y_{i}| \ \ j>i \\
\end{gather*}


we are going to show that the elimination step preserves the strictly dominant property


\begin{gather*}
\vert y_j - y_1 \frac{x_j}{x_1} \vert \ge
\vert y_j\vert - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert} = (1)\\
%
\sum_{i\neq j} \vert y_i - y_1 \frac{x_i}{x_1}\vert \le
\sum_{i\neq j,i\neq 1} \vert y_i\vert  + \sum_{i\neq j,i\neq 1}\vert y_1\vert \frac{\vert x_i\vert}{\vert x_1\vert} =\\
%
(2) + (3)+\vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert} - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}=(4)\\
%
(3)+\vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}=
\vert y_1\vert \frac{\sum_{i\neq 1} \vert x_i\vert}{\vert x_1\vert}<\vert y_1\vert\\
%
(4)<(2)+\vert y_1 \vert - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}=\\
%
\sum_{i\neq j} \vert y_i\vert - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}<(1)
\end{gather*}

title: linsys/concepts_draft
text:
setting up an appropriate SLE is the hardest part of the problem solving…but here we only deal with the “how to solve it” part.
our aim is to solve systems of linear equations (SLE’s)
we have m equations n variables, a cofficient matrix A and a right hand side b 
\begin{gather*}
a_{11}x_{1}+\ldots+a_{1n}x_{n} = b_{1}\\
a_{21}x_{1}+\ldots+a_{2n}x_{n} = b_{2}\\
\vdots{}\\
a_{m1}x_{1}+\ldots+a_{mn}x_{n} = b_{m}\\
\text{or}\\
Ax=b\\
\text{or}\\
x_{1}a_{.1}+x_{2}a_{.2}+\ldots+x_{n}a_{.n}=b\\
\end{gather*}

here by a_{.j} we mean the j-th column of A
the third form is the most expressive one

it tells us that we are working in vector spaces
it tells us that we are searching for a linear combination of the column vectors that is b and the solution for the system (if any) is coefficiant set of the combination.
from linear algebra we know that we have three cases:

we cannot express b by means of the given system (0 solutions)
we can express b uniquely (1-solution)
we can express b in several different ways (\infty-solutions)

matlab

the function call [L,U,P]=lu(A) will create a PLU factorization for the parameter matrix. Almost always row-swaps will be performed.
the backslash (\) operator is a built-in for solving linear systems.







Noneclick to open





>> A=[4,1,-1;-1,8,0;1,1,4];b=[1;2;4];
>> x=A\b;
>> x

x =

    0.3830
    0.2979
    0.8298

ans =

    1.0000
    2.0000
    4.0000




it is important that the \ can return a non-solution. It tries to find the best possible answer and if there is no solution then it tries to minimize \Vert Ax - b\Vert_{2} (least square optimal solution):







Noneclick to open

ans =

title: leastsquares/GN
text:
\Vert f-Ax \Vert_{2}^{2} \ \ \text{is minimal} \ \ \Longleftrightarrow \ \ A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f




a mechanic calculation
take the partial derivatives of J by x_k’s
solve the system \frac{\partial}{\partial x_k}J=0
the “solution” is A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f
A^{T}A is positive semi-definite matrix: the solution is a local minimizer.





it provides more insight to the background of the least squares-method
if Ax=f is solvable then the statement is trivial
lemma: if \langle y,Ax\rangle=0\ \ (\forall x) \ \ \Longrightarrow\ \ A^{T}y=0
let’s assume that f does not lie in the columns space of A
it follows from the Gram-Schmidt method that there is an \hat{x} for which f-A\hat{x} is orthogonal to an arbitrary Ax
A\hat{x} is called orthogonal projection (…)
using the lemma one can prove that A^{T}A\hat{x}=A^{T}f
using the Pythagorean theorem

it follows that A\hat{x} is the unique minimizer of \Vert f-Ax\Vert_{2}.
if f-Ay is orthogonal to an arbitrary Ax for some Ay then Ay=A\hat{x}.

we already know that \hat{x} minimizes J and it is a solution for A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f.
using the lemma again one can prove that if A^{T}Ay=A^{T}f for a y then Ay=A\hat{x}, that is it also y also a minimizer for J.

polynom representations

it is the most generic representation


\begin{gather*}
P(t)=a_n(t-t_{n,1})\ldots(t-t_{n,n})+a_{n-1}(t-t_{n-1,1})\ldots(t-t_{n-1,n-1})+\ldots+\\
a_2(t-t_{2,1})(t-t_{2,2})+a_1(t-t_{1,1})+a_0
\end{gather*}


the main coefficient is the same for each possible representation (why?)



Neville-formula

it tells us how the “larger” P_{1,2,\ldots,k+1} polynomial depends on the “smaller” one P_{1,2,\ldots,k} and P_{2,\ldots,k+1}.


P_{1,2,\ldots,k+1}(t)=\frac{1}{t_1-t_{k+1}}
\left|
\begin{array}{cc}
P_{1,2,\ldots,k}(t) & P_{2,3,\ldots,k+1}(t)\\
t-t_1 & t-t_{k+1}
\end{array}
\right|


proof: evaluate at t_1 and at t_2,\ldots,t_{k} and at t_{k+1} and use the Lagrange main theorem.
it is also valid for [j+1,\ldots, j+k+1] and [j+1,\ldots, j+k] and [j+2,\ldots, j+k+1] for each possible j
let A,B,C denote the main coefficient of the polynomials (in order). then


A=\frac{B-C}{t_1-t_{k+1}}



the scheme and the rule

let’s focus on the upper edge
use induction
(base1): the f^{(0)} column contains the coefficients for the sub-problems: [1],[2],\ldots,[n].

we could start the induction here

(base2): the first computed column contains the main coefficients for each sub-problems: [1,2],[2,3],\ldots,[n-1,n]. (these ar the slopes of the lines)

it means that for 2-sized sub-problems the small triangles’ upper edges are the appropriate coefficients
the induction could be started from here too

(step): consider the subproblem: [1,\ldots,k+1] and the two child problems: [1,\ldots,k] and [2,\ldots,k+1].

on the one hand: P_{1,\ldots,k+1}(t)=P_{1,\ldots,k}(t)+A(t-t_1)\ldots(t-t_k) for some C
on the other hand, from the Neville formula A=\frac{B-C}{t_1-t_{k+1}} - in accordance with the rule used.

the lower edge polynomials/relations can be handled by reversing the order of the points.

set p_k(x)=\frac{P_{k}(x)}{P_{k}(t_{k})}.
set P=\sum_{k=1}^n f_{k}p_{k}

title: interpolation/Lagrange
text:
existence

let \omega_{k}(t)=\prod_{i=1}^k (t-t_i)
start with P_{1}(t)=f_1
P_{k+1}(t)=P_{k}(t)+b_{k+1}\omega_{k}(t)

it is ok for the “old” points (as \omega vanishes at them)
P_{k+1}(t_{k+1})=f_{k+1}
b_{k+1}=\frac{f_{k+1}-P_{k}(t_{k+1})}{\omega_{k}(t_{k+1})} and it is well defined and \deg P_{k+1}\le k

title: interpolation/Lagrange
text:
uniqueness

same as in the classical proof

title: interpolation/Lagrange
text:
existence and uniqueness

P(t)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x_{k} t^k
P(t_k)=f_k\ \ k=1\ldots n is an n \times n linear system (Ax=f)
if Ac=0 for some c, then by setting Q(t)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} c_{k} t^k, Q(t) has n roots (the t’s are different!), but \deg Q\le n-1, consequently c=0 (see this).
it means that A is invertible, that is the system is uniquely solvable.

text:
review

in this section x,y \in \mathbb{R}^n and \alpha \in \mathbb{R}
how large is a vector?
it is denoted by \Vert x \Vert
requirements (axioms):

\Vert x\Vert\ge 0 and \Vert x\Vert=0\ \text{ iff }\ x=0
\Vert x+y \Vert \le \Vert x\Vert + \Vert y \Vert

it is the triangle inequality

\Vert \alpha \cdot x \Vert = \vert \alpha\vert \cdot \Vert x \Vert

the following vector norms are important

\Vert x \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{\vert x_1\vert^2 + \ldots + \vert x_n\vert^2}\ \  (euclidean or 2-norm)
\Vert x \Vert_{1} = \vert x_1\vert + \ldots + \vert x_n\vert\ \  (manhattan or 1-norm)
\Vert x \Vert_{\infty} = \max_{i=k\ldots n}\vert x_k\vert\ \  (\infty-norm)

the distance between vectors: \Vert x-y\Vert

small: close to each other
large: far from each other

1-norm

\Vert A\Vert _1=\max\limits_{1\leq j\leq n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^n\vert
a_{ij}\vert


for each column compute the sum of absolute values and take the maximal one



2 norm

\Vert A\Vert _2=\sqrt{\lambda _{\max} (A^{T}A)}


take the square root of the maximal absolute value eigenvalue of the matrix (A^{T}A)



\infty norm

\Vert A\Vert _\infty=\max_{1\leq i\leq n}\sum_{j=1}^n\vert a_{ij}\vert


for each row compute the sum of absolute values and take the maximal one

title: elsőrendű lineáris homogén
differenciálás
egyben
text:
elsőrendű lineáris homogén






Noney'=A(t)y






   y=Ce^{\int A(t)\text{d}t} \ \ \ \  C\in\mathbb{R}


speciálisan, ha A(t)=A konstans:


   y=Ce^{At} \ \ \ \ C\in\mathbb{R}










Noney'+10y=0






y=Ce^{-10t}\hspace{1cm} C\in\mathbb{R}










Noney'=\log(t)y






\begin{gather*}
   \int \log(t)\text{d}t=\log(t)t-\int 1\text{d}t=\\
   =\log(t)t-t+C \ \ \ \  C\in\mathbb{R}\\
   y=Ce^{t(\log(t)-1)} \ \ \ \  C\in\mathbb{R}
\end{gather*}







Nonematlab





>> syms y(t)
>> disp(int(log(t)))
t*(log(t) - 1)
 
>> disp(dsolve(diff(y)==y(t)*log(t)))
C1*exp(t*(log(t) - 1))








elsőrendű lineáris inhomogén






Noney'=A(t)y+B(t)






konstans variálása
a homogén általános megoldása: Ce^{\int A(t)\text{d}t}

ez konkrét feladatnál már nem integrálos alakban van

ebben a C-t egy ismeretlen C(t) függvénynek tekintjük és behelyettesítjük az inhomogénbe:


\begin{gather*}
C'(t)e^{\int A(t)\text{d}t} + {\color{blue}C(t)e^{\int A(t)\text{d}t}A(t)} = {\color{blue}A(t)C(t)e^{\int A(t)\text{d}t}}+B(t)\\
C'(t) = B(t)e^{-\int A(t)\text{d}t}\\
\end{gather*}


a végén egy sima integrálás marad










Noney'=y+x+2






\begin{gather*}
y=Ce^{x}\ \ \ \text{a homogén megoldása}\\
C=C(x)\ \ \ \text{konstans variálása}\\
C'(x)e^{x}+C(x)e^{x}=C(x)e^{x}+x+2\\
C'(x)=(x+2)e^{-x}\ \ \ \text{parciálisan integráljuk}\\
C(x)=-(x+2)e^{-x}+\int e^{-x}\text{d}x=-(x+3)e^{-x}+K\\
y=\left(-(x+3)e^{-x}+K\right)e^x\\
\end{gather*}







Nonematlab





>> syms y(x)
>> disp(int((x+2)*exp(-x)))
-exp(-x)*(x + 3)
 
>> disp(dsolve(diff(y)==y(x)+x+2))
C1*exp(x) - x - 3












Nonexy'-2y=2x^4






\begin{gather*}
y'=\frac{2}{x}y+2x^3\\
y=Ce^{\int \frac{2}{x}\text{d}x}=Ce^{2\log(x)}=Cx^2\ \ \ \text{a homogén megoldása}\\
C=C(x)\ \ \ \text{konstans variálása}\\
C'(x)x^2+C(x)2x=\frac{2}{x}C(x)x^2+2x^3\\
C'(x)=2x\\
C(x)=x^2+K\\
y=(x^2+K)x^2\\
\end{gather*}







Nonematlab





syms y(x)
>> disp(dsolve(diff(y)==2/x*y+2*x^3))
x^4 + C1*x^2








másodrendű lineáris konstans-együtthatós homogén






NoneAy''+By'+Cy=0






karakterisztikus polinom: Ax^2+Bx+C gyökei szerint osztályozva:
\lambda_1\neq \lambda_2 valósak 
y(t)=K_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}+K_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}

\lambda_1=\lambda_2=\lambda


y(t)=K_1 e^{\lambda t}+K_2te^{\lambda t}


\lambda_{1,2}=a\pm bi komplex


y(t)=e^{at}\left(K_1 \cos(bt)+K_2 \sin(bt)\right)


K_1,K_2\in \mathbb{R} mindenhol










Noney''-y'-2y=0





>> syms y(x)
solve(x^2-x-2)
 
ans =
 
-1
 2
 
>> dsolve(diff(y,2)==diff(y,1)+2*y)
 
ans =
 
C1*exp(-x) + C2*exp(2*x)









Noney''-2y'+y=0





>> syms y(x)
>> solve(x^2-2*x+1)
 
ans =
 
1
1
 
>> dsolve(diff(y,2)==2*diff(y,1)-y)
 
ans =
 
C1*exp(x) + C2*x*exp(x)









Noney''+y'+y=0





>> syms y(x)
>> solve(x^2+x+1)
 
ans =
 
- (3^(1/2)*1i)/2 - 1/2
  (3^(1/2)*1i)/2 - 1/2
 
>> dsolve(diff(y,2)==-diff(y,1)-y)
 
ans =
 
C1*exp(-x/2)*cos((3^(1/2)*x)/2) - C2*exp(-x/2)*sin((3^(1/2)*x)/2)









Noney''+6y'+9y=0,\ \ y(0)=1,\ y'(0)=1











Nonematlab





>> syms y(x)
>> solve(x^2+6*x+9)
 
ans =
 
-3
-3

>> dsolve(diff(y,2)==-6*diff(y,1)-9*y)
 
ans =
 
C1*exp(-3*x) + C2*x*exp(-3*x) 

>> dy=diff(y)
 
dy(x) =
 
diff(y(x), x)
 
>> dsolve(diff(y,2)==-6*diff(y,1)-9*y,y(0)==1,dy(0)==1)
 
ans =
 
exp(-3*x)*(4*x + 1)




\begin{gather*}
y(0)=1\ \ \implies K_1=1\\
y'(0)=1\ \ \implies -3K_1+K_2=1 \ \ \implies K_2=4\\
y(t)=e^{-3x}+4xe^{-3x}
\end{gather*}






Bernoulli






Noney'+g(x)y+h(x)y^a=0\ \ a\neq 1






elsőrendű lineárisra vezetjük vissza


\begin{gather*}
y'+g(x)y+h(x)y^a=0 \ \ \ \ \ [*(1-a)y^{-a}]\\
(1-a)y'y^{-a}+(1-a)g(x)y^{1-a}+(1-a)h(x)=0\ \ \ \ \ [z=y^{1-a}]\\
z'+(1-a)g(x)z+(1-a)h(x)
\end{gather*}










Nonexy^2y'=x^2+y^3






\begin{gather*}
xy^2y'-y^3-x^2=0\\
y'-\frac{1}{x}y-xy^{-2}=0\ \ \ \ a=-2,\ z=y^3\\
z'-\frac{3}{x}z-3x=0
\end{gather*}


konstans variálással


\begin{gather*}
z'=\frac{3}{x}z+3x\\
z=Ce^{\int \frac{3}{x}\text{d}x}=Ce^{3\log(x)}=Cx^3\ \ \ \text{a homogén megoldása}\\
C=C(x)\ \ \ \text{konstans variálása}\\
C'(x)x^3+C(x)3x^2=\frac{3}{x}C(x)x^3+3x\\
C'(x)=\frac{3}{x^2}\\
C(x)=-\frac{3}{x}+K\\
z=(-\frac{3}{x}+K)x^3\\
y=\left((-\frac{3}{x}+K)x^3\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}
\end{gather*}







Nonematlab






először az eredetit nézzük meg (a komplex-szel ne foglalkozzunk)
majd a lineárisat is

>> syms y(x)
>> dsolve(diff(y)==y/x + x/y^2)
 
ans =
 
-3^(1/3)*x^(2/3)*((3^(1/2)*1i)/2 + 1/2)*(C1*x - 1)^(1/3)
 3^(1/3)*x^(2/3)*((3^(1/2)*1i)/2 - 1/2)*(C1*x - 1)^(1/3)
                        3^(1/3)*x^(2/3)*(C1*x - 1)^(1/3)
 
>> dsolve(diff(y)==3/x*y + 3*x)
 
ans =
 
C1*x^3 - 3*x^2








magasabbrendű egyenlet -> első-rendű egyenletrendszer






Noney^{(4)}-y'=0






\begin{gather*}
y_0=y\\
y_1=y'\\
y_2=y''\\
y_3=y'''\\
y_0'=y_1\\
y_1'=y_2\\
y_2'=y_3\\
y_3'=y_1\\
\end{gather*}










None2xy''+y'=0






\begin{gather*}
y_0=y\\
y_1=y'\\
\hline{}
y_0'=y_1\\
y_1'=-\frac{y_1}{2x}\\
\end{gather*}










Nonexy'''-y''-xy'+y=0






\begin{gather*}
y_0=&y\\
y_1=&y'\\
y_2=&y''\\
\hline{}
y_0'=&y_1\\
y_1'=&y_2\\
y_2'=&\frac{-y_0+xy_1+y_2}{x}
\end{gather*}






fokozatos közelítés






Noney'=f(t,y)\ \ \ \Leftrightarrow \ \ y(t)=y(t_0)+\int_{t_0}^t f(s,y(s))\text{d}s






a jobboldalba belerakva y-t visszakapjuk azt \to fixpont
ha f “jó” tulajdonságú akkor a fixpont-iteráció bármilyen kezdő helyről (pont/függvény) indulva a fixponthoz (ami itt egy függvény) kerül egyre közelebb.

\end{cases}
